1:
“Now a certain man was sick, named Lazarus, of Bethany, the town of Mary and her sister Martha.” The witnesses open the chapter that, Augustine says, “holds a foremost place in preaching” among the miracles. The Latin fathers set the tone. Augustine bids us “rejoice rather than wonder,” for “a man was raised up by Him who made man… it is a greater deed to create men than to raise them again from the dead.” Bede notes that “Lazarus signifies helped,” and “of all the dead which our Lord raised, he was most helped, for he had lain dead four days.” The Eastern Chrysostom draws the pastoral lesson at once: “many men, when they see any of those who are pleasing to God suffering… are offended,” not knowing “that to those especially dear to God it belongeth to endure these things” — for “Lazarus also was one of the friends of Christ, and was sick.”

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting and a notable question. Robertson parses the periphrastic imperfect “was sick,” identifies Lazarus as a shortened Eleazar, and locates Bethany “about two miles east of Jerusalem on the south-east slope of Olivet,” now “called El Azariyeh, from the name Lazarus.” Lightfoot adds the rabbinic forms of the names. Barnes raises the question why John alone records so great a miracle, and answers with the most probable reason: “at the time they wrote Lazarus was still living,” and to record it “might have exposed Lazarus to opposition and persecution” (John 12:10–11); John, writing later and for Christians outside Palestine, could relate it fully. Ellicott notes the link with the parable of the rich man and Lazarus (Luke 16:20), whose picture of the world beyond the grave may have been suggested by this very history.

Calvin, for the Reformed, names the chapter’s great theme: in raising Lazarus Christ “placed before our eyes a lively image of our future resurrection.” This was “the latest and concluding action of his life,” the time of his death “already at hand,” so that he “illustrated his own glory in an extraordinary manner” — displaying his power, beyond his earlier raisings, “on a rotting corpse.” Wesley adds the tradition that Lazarus “was now thirty years old, and lived thirty years after Christ’s ascension.” The witnesses thus open the narrative with both its wonder and its meaning: the Creator stooping to raise his sick friend, as a pledge and image of the resurrection of all his own.
2:
“(It was that Mary which anointed the Lord with ointment, and wiped his feet with her hair, whose brother Lazarus was sick.)” The witnesses treat John’s parenthesis as an identifying note and debate its time-reference. The grammatical-historical commentators agree the clause distinguishes this Mary from others of the name. Robertson and Ellicott read it as “an evident proleptic allusion” to the anointing John himself will narrate in John 12:3: writing late, “as John looks back from the end of the century it was all behind him,” though the deed “took place after the events in chapter 11” — the aorist participle being “timeless,” picturing the act. Lightfoot dissents, urging that the aorist “anointed” should “have its full force” and refer to a past event, since Lazarus had not yet been mentioned while Mary was already known; on his view the reference is to an earlier anointing. Most, however, follow the proleptic reading, taking the well-known deed of John 12:3 as named here by anticipation.

The commentators are firmer in rejecting two false identifications. Calvin calls it “a display of ignorance” to imagine “that this Mary was that woman of wicked and infamous life” of Luke 7:37; for “Christ was anointed on various occasions, and even at different places” — the sinner anointed him at Jerusalem, but Mary “afterwards at Bethany, which was her own village.” Ellicott agrees there is “no sufficient reason for identifying Mary of Bethany with the woman which was a sinner,” nor with Mary Magdalene. The witnesses thus read the parenthesis as John’s gentle reminder, for readers who knew the gospel story, of who this Mary was — the woman whose deed of love “should be told for a memorial… wheresoever the gospel” was preached (Matthew 26:13) — and they guard her from the confusions that later tradition would heap upon her.

The witnesses note that even this small editorial touch reveals John’s pastoral care for his readers. By naming Mary through her best-known act of devotion, he both fixes which Mary is meant and quietly honours the love that had poured out the costly ointment. The note thus binds the two scenes — the sister who would anoint the Lord for his burial, and the brother whom the Lord would raise from the grave — into a single household marked, above all, by love for Christ.
3:
“Therefore his sisters sent unto him, saying, Lord, behold, he whom thou lovest is sick.” The witnesses dwell on the delicacy and faith of the message. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the sisters “sent” rather than went — being women, with their sick brother to tend, and Jesus at some distance (Gill). Clarke admires the message as “nothing… more simple, nor more modest”: “they do not say, Come and heal him… they content themselves with simply stating the case, and using an indirect but a most forcible argument” — “He is sick, and thou lovest him; therefore thou canst neither abandon him, nor us.” Ellicott hears “the touching simplicity of the message just as they were sent by the sorrowing sisters”; “they feel that the sad news needs no addition.” Barnes draws from the whole scene that “special attachments are lawful for Christians,” and that the friendships “sweetened with the spirit of Christ” are “peculiarly lovely.”

The Eastern Chrysostom (commenting from the chapter’s opening) reads in their sending “great devotion and faith”: like the centurion and the nobleman, they “sent, not went, to Christ; partly from their great faith… partly because their sorrow kept them at home.” And their wording betrays their high thought of his power — “such was their idea of our Lord’s power, that they were surprised that one whom He loved could be seized with sickness.” The witnesses thus find in this brief message a model of believing prayer: not dictating to Christ what he must do, but laying the need before him and resting on his love. The sisters trust that the bond of affection — “he whom thou lovest” — is itself the surest plea, and leave the manner and the timing of help wholly to him.

The witnesses also observe what the sisters leave unsaid. They do not presume to prescribe a remedy, nor even to ask outright for healing; they simply name the bond of love and the fact of need, and trust Christ to act as that love directs. In this they teach the church how to pray — to spread our wants before the Lord and rest in his affection, rather than to dictate the time or the manner of his help. The plea ‘he whom thou lovest’ is stronger than any argument we could frame.
4:
“When Jesus heard that, he said, This sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of God, that the Son of God might be glorified thereby.” The witnesses unfold both clauses — the riddle of “not unto death,” and the great end of God’s glory. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the sickness was “not unto death” not in the sense that Lazarus would not die, but that death would not be its final issue: it was “not to death, but to give occasion for a miracle” (Augustine), so that physical death became the very means of a greater glory. Chrysostom presses the grammar of “that”: it “signifies not the cause, but the event” — “the sickness sprang from natural causes, but He turned it to the glory of God.” Ellicott and Robertson read the double end — “the glory of God” and the glorifying of “the Son of God” — as inseparable, the Father glorified in the Son’s power over death.

The Latin Augustine fastens on the deity hidden in the saying. The sickness is “for the glory of God… that the Son of God might be glorified thereby” — wherein “our Lord calls Himself God by implication, thus confounding those heretics who say that the Son of God is not God.” For the glory of which God? — of the Son, who is glorified by the miracle. Calvin, for the Reformed, gathers the comfort: the saying teaches that the afflictions of God’s people are ordered not to their destruction but to the manifesting of God’s glory and, through it, their good. The witnesses thus find the verse setting the whole story in its true light before it unfolds: the death of Lazarus, real and grievous as it will be, is from the first appointed not as an end but as an occasion — that God may be glorified, and the Son of God shown to be the Lord of life. What looks like the triumph of death is staged for the triumph of the Author of resurrection.

The witnesses draw the practical comfort. If the sickness of one whom Christ loved was ordered ‘for the glory of God,’ then the believer may read his own afflictions in the same light: not as signs of abandonment, but as occasions appointed for the glory of God and the deepening of faith. The verse does not deny that Lazarus will truly die; it declares that death itself is enlisted in a higher purpose, and that what the sisters dread is already, in the counsel of Christ, the stage for a greater revelation of his power.
5:
“Now Jesus loved Martha, and her sister, and Lazarus.” The witnesses treat this brief verse as the hinge on which the whole strange delay turns, for it stands immediately before the notice that Jesus “abode two days still in the same place.” The grammatical-historical commentators stress that the love was real and particular: the evangelist names each of the three, and the verb (Robertson, Ellicott) is the deliberate agapaō, the love of settled regard. This family was, Barnes had said, among “the few peculiar and intimate friends of our Lord” (Luke 10:38–42). The order of the names — Martha first, then “her sister,” then Lazarus — the commentators take as the order of the household, Martha apparently the elder.

The point the witnesses press is the deliberate placing of this verse. John records the love precisely here, Calvin notes, so that we may not stumble at the delay that follows: that Christ “loved” them and yet “abode two days still in the same place” (v.6) shows that his love is not measured by haste, and that he may withhold present help for a greater good. The delay is no failure of affection but its servant; he loves them too well to spare them the sorrow through which he means to glorify God and deepen their faith. So the verse, far from being a mere note of sentiment, is the key to the whole narrative: every step of the slowness that follows is to be read in the light of this settled, costly love. What seems neglect is love working toward resurrection.

The witnesses also note the tenderness of the threefold naming. Christ’s love is not a vague benevolence but a particular affection for Martha, for Mary, and for Lazarus, each named in turn. This is the love that will weep at the grave (v.35) even as it purposes to raise the dead; and it is recorded here so that nothing in the delay or the sorrow to come may be mistaken for indifference. The whole chapter is to be read as the working out of this love — a love strong enough to let those it cherishes pass through death, that it may show itself stronger than the grave.
6:
“When he had heard therefore that he was sick, he abode two days still in the same place where he was.” The witnesses read the strange delay in the light of the love just declared (v.5), and find in it deliberate purpose. The Eastern Chrysostom and the Latin Alcuin give the plainest reason: he waited so that Lazarus should die and be buried, “that they might say, he stinks, and none doubt that it was death, and not a trance, from which he was raised” — Alcuin adds, “that the recovery might be a more wonderful one.” Augustine reads the going that follows as the rhythm of the incarnation: “He left indeed as man… He left in weakness, but He returns in power.”

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the facts and weigh the motive. Barnes reckons that “Lazarus died soon after the messengers left him,” and that Jesus “did not hasten… that there might not be the possibility of doubt that he was dead,” so that he had lain four days in the grave when Christ arrived (v.39); the very delay, he adds, “shows that he intended to raise him up,” for otherwise it could “hardly be reconciled with friendship.” Robertson notes the two days “seemed unlike Jesus to the sisters.” Ellicott sets aside the common explanations — that the delay was to test their faith (already shown in their message) or because of work he would not leave — and grounds it instead in the principle Christ had taught from the first miracle (John 2:4): the hours of his work are marked by the Father, not by human urgency.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the comfort from the placing of the verse. That Christ loved them and yet delayed teaches that his love does not always answer at once, and that he may withhold present help for a greater end. The witnesses thus agree the two days’ delay is no coldness but design: love and wisdom together hold him back, that the death of Lazarus may be beyond all dispute, and the glory of his rising beyond all doubt. What the sisters could only feel as an aching silence was, in the counsel of Christ, the careful preparing of the stage on which his power over death would be displayed.
7:
“Then after that he saith to his disciples, Let us go into Judæa again.” The witnesses note both the resolve and the danger it courts. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the timing — Robertson’s “thereafter… after the two days had elapsed” — and the volitive “let us go” (agōmen). The word “again,” Ellicott observes, “recalls the dangers from which they had escaped at the close of their last visit to Jerusalem” (John 10:39); they had but lately fled “the rage of the Jews” to “this haven in Bethany beyond Jordan” (John 10:40; Robertson). Calvin, for the Reformed, hears in the summons the answer to the disciples’ fear that Christ “had forgotten” Lazarus, or reckoned other things “of more importance than the life of Lazarus”: now “he shows that he cared about” him, and bids them cross the Jordan.

Henry, for the Puritans, draws the lessons that govern the whole exchange. “Christ never brings his people into any danger but he goes with them in it,” and “our day shall be lengthened out, till our work is done, and our testimony finished.” He turns it to self-examination: “we are apt to think ourselves zealous for the Lord, when really we are only zealous for our wealth, credit, ease, and safety; we have therefore need to try our principles.” The comfort of duty is that “a man has comfort and satisfaction while in the way of his duty, as set forth by the word of God, and determined by the providence of God.” The witnesses thus find in the brief command a quiet courage: Christ deliberately turns back toward the place of his enemies, not in recklessness but in obedience to the Father and love for his friend, and calls his fearful disciples to follow him into the danger he will share.

The witnesses note the courage hidden in so plain a sentence. The disciples had thought Christ was lingering far from the danger, perhaps glad of the respite; instead he proposes to walk straight back into the country where they had just sought his life. The command thus reveals both his fearless devotion to his friend and his perfect submission to the Father’s timing — he goes not because the danger has passed, but because the hour for this work has come.
8:
“His disciples say unto him, Master, the Jews of late sought to stone thee; and goest thou thither again?” The witnesses hear in the question both genuine alarm and a hidden self-concern. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the reference: the stoning attempt was recent — “but a few weeks before… on the day of the feast of the dedication” (Clarke; John 10:31). Ellicott renders it “the Jews were just now seeking to stone Thee,” and notes “they think of the danger to Him, and are not without thought of the danger to themselves” (v.16); the hatred had made it “unsafe for our Lord and His disciples to be within their reach,” the retirement in Gaulonitis evidently brief. Robertson, parsing the conative imperfect “were but now seeking,” adds bluntly that to the disciples “it seemed suicidal madness to go back now.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, probes the motive beneath the plea. The disciples dissuade him “not so much perhaps on his account as on their own, for each of them is alarmed about himself, as the danger was common to all.” Avoiding the cross, and “ashamed to own it, they allege — what is more plausible — that they are anxious about their Master.” He turns it sharply on the conscience: “the same thing happens every day with many,” who “through a dread of the cross shrink from the performance of their duty,” and “eagerly seek excuses to conceal their indolence, that they may not be thought to rob God of the obedience due to him.” The witnesses thus find in the disciples’ objection a familiar human mixture — real affection for Christ entangled with real fear for themselves — and a warning against dressing up cowardice as prudence or care for others.

The witnesses observe how naturally the disciples’ fear masks itself as concern. They speak of the peril to their Master, but their alarm is also for themselves, and they would gladly keep him in the safe retreat beyond Jordan. Their question is therefore a mirror in which every reluctant follower may see himself — quick to find a pious reason for declining a hard duty, and slow to own that the real obstacle is the fear of the cross.
9:
“Jesus answered, Are there not twelve hours in the day? If a man walk in the day, he stumbleth not, because he seeth the light of this world.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s serene answer to their fear, grounded in the appointed span of his life. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the Jewish reckoning of the day “from sunrise to sunset into twelve equal parts” (Barnes, Gill), and the figure of a traveller who, walking by daylight, “stumbleth not… he sees the obstacles or dangers and can avoid them.” The day, Clarke notes, “points out the time of life,” the night “that of death,” the same image Christ used at John 9:4. Ellicott draws the application: their dreaded “darkness of the night… could not come yet,” for “the day of His life is marked out by limits no less sure” than the twelve hours; until his appointed hour, he and they “must do the work which is appointed of the Father.” Robertson is careful to deny fatalism: “it is not blind fatalism… but the opposite of cowardice,” Christ having “full confidence in the Father’s purpose about his hour, which has not yet come.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, sets aside fanciful readings (such as that men change their minds “every hour”) for “the simple and natural meaning”: as a traveller by sunlight goes safely while one in the dark stumbles, so Christ walks securely within the time God has allotted him. Wesley puts the sense into Christ’s mouth: “there is such a space, a determined time, which God has allotted me; during that time I stumble not, amidst all the snares that are laid for me,” for “I see the light of God surrounding me.” The witnesses thus find the answer turning the disciples’ fear on its head: not recklessness, but the calm certainty that his enemies cannot shorten his day by an hour, so that to do the Father’s will — even in Judea — is the safest place on earth until his work is done.

The witnesses add that the answer rebukes the disciples’ premise without scorning their love. They reasoned as though going to Judea were to throw his life away; Christ replies that his life, like the day, has its fixed measure, and that no malice can cut it short before the Father wills. Far from courting death, he is simply refusing to let fear keep him from the work of the daylight hours — and he calls them to the same confidence, that the path of duty is the path of safety.
10:
“But if a man walk in the night, he stumbleth not, because there is no light in him.” The witnesses take this companion clause to move from the figure to its spiritual depth. The grammatical-historical commentators read the first half still of natural night and its perils — the traveller without daylight who “stumbleth” for want of seeing his way (Gill, Barnes; cf. Ecclesiastes 2:14). Ellicott marks the turn: “he passes in this verse from the material to the spiritual truth,” for the clause “holds, too, of the darkness in which men walk who do not see… the light of heaven falling upon the moral path.” The reason given — “because there is no light in him” — shifts the lamp from outside to within: the light is now “not that of this world, but that which is within man.” Robertson agrees Jesus here “pictures spiritual darkness, the worst of all” (cf. Matthew 6:23; John 8:12), and draws the great distinction: “man has the capacity for light, but is not the source of light.”

Wesley reads it of providence and grace together: to “walk in the night” is to be without “light from God,” when his “providence does no longer protect.” The witnesses thus find the verse completing Christ’s answer with a warning and a promise. So long as he walks in the day of the Father’s appointed will, with the light of heaven upon his path, no enemy can make him stumble; but to walk apart from that light — to act in the darkness of one’s own will, with “no light in him” — is to fall. The lesson reaches past Christ to the disciples and to every believer: safety lies not in avoiding danger but in keeping within the light of God’s purpose, for the man who has the inner Light cannot finally stumble, and the man who lacks it cannot finally stand.

The witnesses thus hear in the paired clauses a quiet summons to walk in the light. The danger is not in Judea but in the dark — in acting apart from the Father’s will, where a man has ‘no light in him’ and must stumble. So Christ answers the disciples’ fear not by minimising the peril but by relocating it: to go where God sends, in the light of his purpose, is safe; to shrink back into the night of self-will is the only true danger.
11:
“Our friend Lazarus sleepeth; but I go, that I may awake him out of sleep.” The witnesses dwell on the tender euphemism and the comfort it carries. The Eastern Chrysostom hears in it a double comforting of the disciples: that “they were not going to Jerusalem, but to Bethany,” and that the errand is gentle — “I am not going to dispute again with the Jews, but to awaken our friend.” The word “our friend,” he adds, is meant “to show how strongly they were bound to go.” Augustine presses the relativity of the word: “it was really true that he was sleeping. To our Lord, he was sleeping; to men who could not raise him again, he was dead,” and “our Lord awoke him… as easily as you awake a sleeper.” The grammatical-historical commentators note that “sleep” (koimaomai) is the familiar New Testament euphemism for the death of believers, so gentle in Christ’s mouth that the disciples mistake it for natural rest (Robertson, Gill); Lightfoot says they “apprehend that the edge of the disease… had been taken off.”

Augustine also weighs the saying of the next verses — “I am glad for your sakes that I was not there, that ye may believe” (v.15) — and reads it not of a faith begun but of a faith deepened: “believe more deeply, more firmly.” Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others draw the abiding consolation: for the believer, death is no more than a sleep from which Christ will wake him; the grave is a bed, and the resurrection a rising at his voice. The witnesses thus find in Christ’s chosen word the whole gospel of the chapter in miniature: that what men call death, he calls sleep, because to him who is the resurrection and the life it is as easy to raise the dead as to rouse a sleeper — and he goes to Bethany not to mourn a loss but to “awake” his friend.

The witnesses note how the gentle word reframes the whole journey. The disciples dread a march toward stoning; Christ speaks only of waking a sleeping friend. By naming death ‘sleep,’ he both softens their fear and quietly asserts his mastery over the grave — for one who can ‘awake’ the dead at will has no more cause to dread death than a man has to dread the bed of a sleeper he means to rouse.
12:
“Then said his disciples, Lord, if he sleep, he shall do well.” The witnesses read the disciples’ reply as an eager misunderstanding, seized as an excuse. The grammatical-historical commentators note that among the Jews sleep in sickness was a hopeful sign: “sleep in sickness is a sign of recovery, because it shows that the violence of the disease has abated” (Barnes, quoting Lightfoot), and Ellicott lists it among “six favourable symptoms,” “a common-place in authors of all periods.” Clarke adds the proverbial Greek saying of Menander that “sleep is the health of every disease.” So they reason that “if he be fallen asleep, he shall be saved” (Ellicott) — Robertson notes sōthēsetai in its original sense of “getting well, safe and sound” — and conclude “there was no need of his going into Judea to restore him.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks both their honesty and their motive. They do not, he says, “craftily or deceitfully turn aside Christ’s words… on the pretence of not understanding”; rather, “thinking that he spoke about sleep, they gladly seize this opportunity of avoiding danger.” Their error is genuine, but they welcome it, for it gives them a reason to keep Christ from the perilous journey. The witnesses thus find in the reply a gentle irony: the disciples, dreading the road to Judea, are quick to take the most comforting reading of Christ’s words, and so turn his tender announcement of death into an argument for staying away. Their literalism is not malice but the slowness of minds still bent on the earthly and the safe — a slowness Christ will patiently correct in the next verses by speaking the plain word they could not hear in the figure.

The witnesses note the deeper irony of the moment. The disciples comfort themselves that sleep means recovery, when in truth Lazarus is already dead; their hopeful reading is the very opposite of the fact. Yet their words unwittingly touch the truth Christ intends, for to him the death of Lazarus is indeed but a sleep from which a sure recovery will come. The misunderstanding thus becomes a small parable of how the carnal mind, grasping at the easy sense, misses both the sorrow and the greater hope that the Lord’s words contain.
13:
“Howbeit Jesus spake of his death: but they thought that he had spoken of taking of rest in sleep.” The witnesses treat John’s clarifying aside as evidence of the disciples’ honest incomprehension. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that the words “forbid the thought that they really understood that Lazarus was dead, but did not wish to seem to know it” (Ellicott): their mistake was real. Ellicott grants that three of them “had heard our Lord apply the word ‘sleep’ to death before” (Matthew 9:24, of Jairus’s daughter), yet this misunderstanding “takes its place with others of a like kind” — as at Matthew 16:6–12 — “showing that the surface meaning of words was that which naturally suggested itself to them.” Robertson notes the rare word koimēsis, used here only in the New Testament (though found of death in Sirach), and that the disciples took it of ordinary “taking rest in sleep.”

The witnesses thus find the verse quietly instructive about the gap between Christ’s meaning and his hearers’ grasp. The figure of death as sleep, so natural and so gentle on his lips, was not yet familiar to them as it would become to the church, and so they heard only the literal sense. John records their error not to shame them but to make plain the truth Christ then states without a figure — and to show, by contrast, how the Lord who calls death “sleep” speaks from a knowledge and a power the disciples did not yet share. Their dullness is the foil against which the plain word “Lazarus is dead,” and the greater word “I am the resurrection,” will shine.

The witnesses also draw a lesson for the reader from the disciples’ confusion. If those who walked daily with Christ could so mistake his plainest figures, we should not wonder that spiritual truths are hard for the natural mind, nor presume that mere familiarity with his words guarantees understanding. The same gentle word ‘sleep,’ which the church would come to cherish as the believer’s name for death, fell flat on ears not yet opened; and only the plain statement, and at last the miracle itself, would teach them what the figure meant.
14:
“Then said Jesus unto them plainly, Lazarus is dead.” The witnesses note Christ’s gracious accommodation to the disciples’ dullness. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the adverb “plainly” (parrēsia, “without metaphor,” Robertson; cf. John 16:29): perceiving they had not grasped the gentle figure of sleep, he now drops it and says “in so many words” what he means (Gill). Ellicott catches the condescension: the deeper truth, “Our friend Lazarus is fallen asleep,” having “conveyed no true meaning to their minds,” he uses instead “words which fall short of that truth, but are the only words which they can understand.” Robertson notes the blunt aorist apethanen, “died.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, dwells on the patience this reveals. “The goodness of Christ was astonishing, in being able to bear with such gross ignorance in the disciples.” And he draws out the Lord’s pedagogy: the very reason Christ “delayed, for a time, to bestow upon them the grace of the Spirit in larger measure, was, that the miracle of renewing them in a moment might be the greater” — their present dullness setting off the later transformation at Pentecost. The witnesses thus find in this short verse a model of the divine teacher’s gentleness: where the figure is missed, he speaks the plain word; where understanding is slow, he does not upbraid but condescends. The same Lord who veils his meaning in the tender “sleepeth” will, when his disciples cannot follow, lay it bare — “Lazarus is dead” — that they may be led, step by patient step, toward the faith his coming miracle will confirm.

The witnesses add that the plain word ‘Lazarus is dead’ also prepares the disciples for what is to come. Christ does not soften the fact but states it, so that when he raises Lazarus they may know past doubting that he was truly dead. The same condescension that meets their dullness with plain speech also lays the foundation for their faith: he tells them the worst, that the wonder of the best may be unmistakable. Thus even the bluntness of the announcement serves the purpose named in the next verse — that they may believe.
15:
“And I am glad for your sakes that I was not there, to the intent ye may believe; nevertheless let us go unto him.” The witnesses unfold the startling joy and its purpose. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the gladness is “not at the fact of death, but at the fact that He was not there” (Ellicott); for “had He been there, Lazarus would not have died” (v.21), and a mere healing “would have added to the work of healing,” but a raising after four days would be far more impressive. Barnes paraphrases: had he been present, “the entreaties of his sisters and friends would have prevailed… to restore him to health,” and he “could not have refused them without appearing to be unkind”; but the greater miracle “will be far more impressive,” so “I rejoice.” Clarke counts the deepening wonders — that Christ knew Lazarus dead though none had told him, that raising the dead is greater than curing the sick, and that raising one four days buried is greater still. Robertson marks the ingressive aorist “that ye may come to believe,” a faith to be increased.

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the principle beneath the joy: Christ’s absence “was profitable to them, because his power would have been less illustriously displayed, if he had instantly given assistance.” For “the more nearly the works of God approach to the ordinary course of nature, the less highly are they valued” — “if God immediately stretches out his hand, we do not perceive his assistance.” That the resurrection “might be acknowledged… to be truly a Divine work, it must be delayed.” The witnesses thus find the verse turning the whole sorrow toward the disciples’ faith: Christ rejoices not in their loss but in the occasion it gives to confirm them, and the “nevertheless” — “let us go unto him” — shows that the same love which delayed for their sakes now moves at last toward the grave, to do for Lazarus, and for their believing, the greater work.

The witnesses underline the tender paradox of the saying. Christ does not rejoice that his friend has died, nor that the sisters grieve; he rejoices that the circumstances are such as will most deeply confirm his disciples’ faith. Love for them governs even his gladness, as love for Lazarus governs the ‘nevertheless, let us go unto him.’ The verse thus binds together the two loves that run through the chapter — for the dead friend and for the living disciples — and shows that the delay which seemed unkind was, from first to last, the working of a wise and far-seeing affection.
16:
“Then said Thomas, which is called Didymus, unto his fellowdisciples, Let us also go, that we may die with him.” The witnesses note Thomas’s loyal courage and its despairing tone, and debate the reference of “him.” The grammatical-historical commentators first explain the name: “Thomas” is Hebrew, “Didymus” its Greek equivalent, both meaning “twin” (Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson), so that he clearly “had a twin brother or sister.” On “that we may die with him,” the commentators divide. Some refer it to Lazarus: “let us go and die, for what have we to hope… they will put him to death, and we also, like Lazarus, shall be dead” (Barnes); on this reading, with a touch of irony, the disciples expect only to share Lazarus’s grave. Others, with Wesley, refer it to Jesus — “with Jesus, whom he supposed the Jews would kill.” Either way, Robertson and Ellicott agree, the saying shows a devotion ready to follow Christ to death.

Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs the spirit of it carefully. Thomas “is now prepared to follow, but it is without confidence; or, at least, he does not fortify himself by the promise of Christ, so as to follow him with cheerfulness and composure.” It is, Calvin says, “the language of despair, for they ought to have entertained no fears about their own life” under the protection of the Lord of the twelve hours (v.9). The witnesses thus find in Thomas a true but flawed loyalty — the same Thomas who will later doubt the resurrection (John 20:24) here resolves to die with his Master, yet resolves gloomily, expecting defeat rather than trusting deliverance. His brave despair is the disciples’ answer to a danger they cannot avoid: unable to dissuade Christ, they will at least perish at his side — love without faith, courage without hope, yet love and courage still.

The witnesses find in Thomas a character true to himself throughout the Gospel. The same man who here will follow Christ even to death, yet expects only death, is the one who will later refuse to believe the resurrection until he touches the wounds; his love is real, his hope dim. His words rally the wavering disciples and shame their reluctance, but they breathe resignation rather than trust. In him the church sees a familiar disciple — devoted enough to die with Christ, but slow to believe that with Christ death itself is not the end.
17:
“Then when Jesus came, he found that he had lain in the grave four days already.” The witnesses attend both to the chronology and to the significance of the four days. The grammatical-historical commentators reconstruct the timing: Chrysostom reckons that the messenger came the day Lazarus died, Jesus “stayed two days,” and the journey filled the rest, “which brings us to the fourth day.” Clarke infers that Lazarus “had been buried about four days,” and notes that, though embalming was the Jewish custom, “he had not been embalmed” (v.39), “God wisely ordering this” so that the corruption should be real and the miracle undoubted. Henry traces the journey itself, on which (the other Gospels tell) Christ healed blind Bartimaeus at Jericho and converted Zacchaeus — “we must not reckon ourselves out of our way, while we are in the way of doing good.”

The Latin fathers read the four days allegorically. Alcuin repeats that the delay made “the resurrection of Lazarus the more glorious.” Augustine finds in the four days four kinds of death — the death every man inherits by birth, the death of transgressing the natural law, the death of despising the written law given by Moses, and the death of transgressing the Gospel — “but Christ doth not disdain to awaken even these.” Alcuin matches them to the stages of sin: “elation of heart… assent… act… habit.” However the number is read, the witnesses agree on its force: four days in the tomb, with corruption already begun (v.39), put the death of Lazarus beyond all question, so that when Christ calls him forth no one can pretend it was a swoon. The longer the grave had held him, the greater the glory of the One who would summon him out of it.

The witnesses gather the force of the detail. The four days are no idle note of time but the measure of an undeniable death: the body had lain long enough for decay to begin, long enough for any thought of a swoon or a trance to be excluded. So the stage is set with deliberate care, that the glory of the coming miracle may rest on a death no one could question. What had seemed to the sisters an unbearable delay proves to be the very thing that makes the resurrection of Lazarus the crowning sign of Christ’s power over the grave.
18:
“Now Bethany was nigh unto Jerusalem, about fifteen furlongs off.” The witnesses read this geographical note as more than incidental — it explains the crowd of witnesses the miracle would have. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the distance: “fifteen furlongs” is “nearly two miles” (Barnes, Clarke), the Greek stadion being about 606 feet; Lightfoot adds that “the Jewish miles did not hold out full eight furlongs… but seven and a half.” Barnes draws the consequence: the nearness made it “easy for many of the Jews to come,” and meant that “the news that Jesus was there, and the account of the miracle, would also be easily carried to the Sanhedrim.” Ellicott notes John’s past tense, “Bethany was nigh,” as a sign of one “writing after the destruction of Jerusalem,” when the buildings and gardens “could no longer be described as still existing.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the providence behind the detail. “The Evangelist diligently follows out all that contributes to the certainty of the narrative.” He relates the nearness of Bethany so that “no one may be astonished” that many came from Jerusalem to comfort the sisters; and though their own “inducement to go” was kindness, “they were assembled there, by a secret decree of God, for another purpose, that the resurrection of Lazarus might not remain unknown,” nor the witnesses be only friends of the family. The witnesses thus find even this small topographical note serving the great design: God so orders the place and the distance that the raising of Lazarus, wrought within two miles of the hostile city, should be done before a multitude of witnesses — including the very Jews who would carry the report to the council that plots his death.

The witnesses note how the small geographical detail underwrites the credibility of the whole account. A miracle wrought in some remote corner might be doubted or unknown; but Bethany lay within an easy walk of the capital, so that the raising of Lazarus could be neither hidden nor hushed. The very proximity that made the place dangerous for Christ made it ideal for the manifestation of his glory — performed, as it were, at the gates of the city that would soon condemn him.
19:
“And many of the Jews came to Martha and Mary, to comfort them concerning their brother.” The witnesses describe the mourning custom and discern God’s purpose in the throng. The grammatical-historical commentators detail the Jewish practice: Clarke notes mourning “lasted about thirty days: the three first days were termed days of weeping, then followed seven of lamentation”; Barnes and Robertson fix “seven days of solemn mourning” (cf. 1 Samuel 31:13), during which friends came to console. Lightfoot describes the comforters standing “in a circle about the mourner.” The presence of “many of the Jews,” Robertson and Ellicott agree, “indicates the prominence of the family,” who, being “of position and substance” (cf. Mark 14:3), “would naturally have had many friends among the higher rank of the Jews” — though these mourners were “probably not hostile to Jesus.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, again reads providence in the scene: comforting the bereaved “was, no doubt, the object which they had in view, but God had another object to accomplish” — to gather witnesses. He adds a pastoral note on grief itself: since “it is natural that the death of friends should occasion grief and mourning to men, this duty… ought not to be blamed, unless on this ground, that sinful excess… corrupts what is not in itself sinful.” The witnesses thus find the verse both true to life and laden with design. The custom of consolation, the prominence of the family, the crowd of Jerusalem mourners — all are real, and all are quietly bent by God toward one end: that when Lazarus comes forth, there shall be no lack of witnesses, friendly and hostile alike, to a death and a resurrection done in the full view of many.

The witnesses add that the crowd of mourners, gathered by custom and kinship, becomes in God’s hand a jury for the miracle. Many who came merely to weep would stay to wonder, and some, unable to deny what they saw, would carry word to the rulers (v.46). Thus the ordinary rites of grief are woven into the divine plan: the consolers of the sisters become the witnesses of the resurrection, and the house of mourning is made the theatre of Christ’s greatest sign before the cross.
20:
“Then Martha, as soon as she heard that Jesus was coming, went and met him: but Mary sat still in the house.” The witnesses see in the two sisters their familiar characters. The grammatical-historical commentators recall that Martha was the one “intrusted with the management of the affairs of the family” (Barnes; cf. Luke 10:40), and so, “active” and “stirring about” (Gill), she “first heard of his coming, and, without waiting to inform her sister, went immediately out to meet him.” Robertson marks the ingressive aorist “went and met him” against the imperfect picture of Mary, who “still sat in the house” — the word for sitting being, Clarke notes, the ancient posture of grief (cf. Job 2:8; Ezekiel 8:14), in which the mourner sat “immovable.”

Ellicott and Calvin add a further reason for Martha’s going out. She meets Jesus “in a place where she can speak her heart’s thoughts, apart from the oppressive ceremonial of the formal lamentation, and where He would not be exposed to a renewal of the attempts against His life” (Ellicott); Calvin notes she “travels beyond the village… that she might meet him more secretly, for his danger was fresh in his recollection.” Wesley suggests Mary, sitting within, was “probably not hearing what was said.” The witnesses thus find the verse true both to the sisters and to the moment: the two temperaments John and Luke alike portray — Martha the active, Mary the contemplative — show themselves even in grief; and the prudence of meeting Christ quietly, away from the watching mourners and the lurking danger, governs the scene. The active sister hastens to her Lord; the sorrowing sister waits, until she too is called.

The witnesses find the scene quietly faithful to all we know of the household. Martha, ever the doer, cannot sit still under sorrow but must be up and out to meet the Lord; Mary, ever the one who sat at his feet, sits now in the stillness of grief until she is summoned. Neither is blamed; each meets Christ in her own way. And the very difference of their temperaments, shown even in mourning, prepares the two distinct encounters the chapter will record, as the Lord deals with each sister according to her need.
21:
“Then said Martha unto Jesus, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.” The witnesses hear in these words faith and longing mingled with a touch of reproach. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Mary will speak the very same words (v.32), so that they had plainly “been spoken more than once by the sisters to each other” (Ellicott), the “utterance of the thought which had already been expressed in their message” (v.3). Robertson parses the second-class condition — “Lord, if thou hadst been here” — and finds in it “wistful longing if not with a bit of reproach for his delay.” Clarke observes that the sisters’ faith, though real, was “not complete”: they “thought he could cure at hand, but not at a distance,” supposing that, had he been present, he would have healed Lazarus. Their conviction of his power, Ellicott says, is stated “in the definiteness of full conviction,” even if its bounds were too narrow.

Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs the saying with characteristic care. Martha “begins with a complaint, though in doing so she modestly expresses her wish”; her meaning is, “by thy presence thou mightst have delivered my brother from death.” He grants that “her words proceeded partly from faith,” but adds that “there were disorderly passions mixed with them, which hurried her beyond due bounds,” so that she “gives way to her feelings, instead of restraining them under the rule of faith.” The witnesses thus find in Martha a portrait of the believer in grief: clinging to Christ, sure of his love and power, yet limiting both by her own narrow expectations and letting sorrow edge toward complaint. Her words are the first of a series in which Christ will gently lead her from a faith bound to the past — “if thou hadst been here” — to a faith that grasps him as the present and living Lord of the resurrection.

The witnesses observe that Martha’s word, for all its wistfulness, is at bottom an act of faith. She does not doubt that Christ could have saved her brother; her grief is that he was not there to do it. The complaint is the complaint of love and trust, not of unbelief — and Christ treats it so, answering not with rebuke but with a promise. In her we see how faith and sorrow may dwell together in the same heart, and how the Lord bears tenderly with a confidence that is real even when it is imperfect.
22:
“But I know, that even now, whatsoever thou wilt ask of God, God will give it thee.” The witnesses hear in this a half-formed hope that dares not speak its full desire. The grammatical-historical commentators read it as Martha’s gentle, indirect intimation that even yet something might be done. Clarke: “she durst not ask so great a favour in direct terms; she only intimated modestly that she knew he could do it.” Barnes agrees she means that “whatever is necessary to our consolation… thou canst obtain,” and thinks “it is possible that she meant gently to intimate that he could raise him up.” Ellicott unfolds the “half-formed hope, which she dare not utter, perhaps dare not even think, that her brother may be restored to life again” — a hope kindled, he suggests, by the memory of Jairus’s daughter (Mark 5:35) and the widow’s son at Nain (Luke 7:11). Robertson notes the better text reads simply “even now I know,” without the adversative “but.”

Yet the witnesses also mark the limit in Martha’s faith. She speaks of Christ as one who must “ask of God,” as a favoured suppliant rather than the Lord of life in his own right — a way of speaking that Christ’s answer (vv.25–26) will at once raise to a higher plane. Her confidence is real and reverent: she believes God refuses him nothing. But it stops short of grasping that the One before her is himself “the resurrection and the life,” who does not merely obtain miracles by prayer but works them by his own power. The verse thus catches Martha mid-way between the faith she has and the faith Christ will draw out of her — trusting his favour with God, not yet owning his Godhead — and so prepares the great self-disclosure that follows.

The witnesses note how Christ will honour and exceed this halting hope. Martha believes God will grant whatever Christ asks; she does not yet see that he need not ask as a petitioner but commands as the Lord of life. His reply will not crush her timid expectation but transform it, leading her from the thought of a favour obtained by prayer to the confession of the One in whom resurrection itself resides. The verse thus marks the furthest reach of her faith before Christ lifts it higher.
23:
“Jesus saith unto her, Thy brother shall rise again.” The witnesses note the deliberate, comforting ambiguity of the promise. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the words could be taken two ways — of the general resurrection at the last day, or of an immediate raising — and that Christ frames them so as to draw out Martha’s faith. Robertson marks the future middle “shall rise again” (anastēsetai) as “designedly” open, a word that “Martha takes… of the final resurrection.” Barnes and Ellicott agree the saying is at first “capable of being understood” in the common Jewish sense, so that Christ meets Martha where her faith is, on the ground of the resurrection she already confesses, before lifting her higher.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it as Christ’s method of leading her on. He gives a promise “of which she might lay hold,” but in terms that test and stretch her understanding; for she will answer (v.24) by retreating to “the resurrection at the last day,” a hope true but distant, and so give Christ occasion to declare himself the resurrection present and at hand. The witnesses thus find the verse a hinge in the dialogue: the bare words “thy brother shall rise again” are both an immediate comfort and a doorway. They confirm the orthodox hope of resurrection, and at the same time, by their very openness, invite Martha to ask whether more is meant — preparing her, and the reader, for the word in which Christ gathers all resurrection into his own person: “I am the resurrection, and the life.”

The witnesses observe the tenderness of the method. Christ does not at once overwhelm the grieving sister with the full claim he is about to make; he gives her first a simple, sure word of hope — ‘thy brother shall rise again’ — and lets her response reveal how far her faith reaches. In this he shows the patience of the good Teacher, who leads the soul forward step by step, meeting it first on familiar ground before opening to it the deeper truth it is not yet ready to see.
24:
“Martha saith unto him, I know that he shall rise again in the resurrection at the last day.” The witnesses read Martha’s answer as the confession of true but distant faith. The grammatical-historical commentators note that she falls back on the orthodox hope of a general resurrection “at the last day,” the doctrine of the Pharisees and of the Scriptures (Gill, Barnes), which she holds firmly (cf. Daniel 12:2; John 5:28–29). Robertson marks the locative “in the resurrection (anastasei) at the last day” as expressing “the orthodox Pharisaic hope.” Ellicott observes that her faith, real and reverent, is yet a faith that pushes its comfort off to the far future: she believes Lazarus will rise, but only “at the last day,” and so does not lay hold of the nearer hope her earlier words had half-breathed (v.22).

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the wavering in her reply. Having “a little before” seemed to reach toward an immediate raising, she now “restricts the grace of God” to the last day, her faith “creeping on the ground.” Yet he is gentle with her: she holds the sound doctrine of the resurrection, and Christ will not rebuke but enlarge it. The witnesses thus find in Martha a believer whose creed is orthodox but whose grasp of Christ is still too small. She knows there will be a resurrection; she does not yet know that the Resurrection is standing before her. Her answer, true as far as it goes, sets the stage for Christ to gather the whole future hope into his own present person — to take her confession of a doctrine and turn it into faith in a Person, in the words that immediately follow.

The witnesses draw the lesson that sound doctrine is not yet living faith. Martha confesses the resurrection truly, as every orthodox Jew did; but a creed held at arm’s length, fixed wholly in the distant future, cannot comfort the present grief or grasp the present Christ. The verse therefore stands as a gentle warning against a faith that is correct but cold, and as the occasion for Christ to do what he ever delights to do — to draw the believer from assent to a doctrine into trust in himself, the living embodiment of all she has confessed.
25:
“Jesus said unto her, I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live.” The witnesses receive this as one of the supreme self-disclosures of the Gospel, gathering all Martha’s future hope into Christ’s present person. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the startling shift from doctrine to Person. Robertson: “they are not mere doctrines about future events, but present realities in Jesus himself,” and quotes Westcott — “the Resurrection is one manifestation of the Life: it is involved in the Life.” Ellicott draws out the emphatic “I”: where Martha had spoken of resurrection as “an event in the far-off future,” Christ teaches that “the resurrection is to be thought of as His person, and… as actually present — ‘I, and none beside Me, am the Resurrection… a present life, and not simply a life in the remoteness of the last day.’” Barnes calls him “the author or the cause of the resurrection… so that it may be said that I am the resurrection itself” (compare 1 Corinthians 1:30). Lightfoot sets the claim against the rabbinic fable that resurrection springs from a little bone in the spine: “our blessed Jesus, who hath raised himself from the dead, is the spring and principle of ours.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, unfolds the order of the two titles. Christ is “the resurrection” first, “because the restoration from death to life naturally comes before the state of life”: “the whole human race is plunged in death… no man will be a partaker of life until he is risen from the dead.” Then he is “the life,” the continuance as well as the beginning, for he “never permits life… to be lost, but preserves it to the end.” The reference, Calvin and Gill agree, is at once to the spiritual resurrection “from a death of sin to a life of grace” and to the bodily raising that pledges it. Wesley sums the two clauses neatly — “the resurrection of the dead, and the life of the living.” The witnesses thus find in this verse the heart of the chapter and the answer to Martha’s narrowed faith: she had confessed a resurrection at the last day; Christ replies that the Resurrection is standing before her, so that the believer, “though he die,” shall live — for life and the raising to it are alike lodged in the person of the Son.
26:
“And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die. Believest thou this?” The witnesses read this companion clause as the second half of Christ’s great promise, balancing the first. Where v.25 spoke of the believing dead who “shall live,” this speaks of the believing living who “shall never die.” The grammatical-historical commentators are careful about the sense of death: Robertson marks the strong double negative “shall by no means die for ever” (ou mē apothanē eis ton aiōna), and notes the death meant here is “spiritual death,” eternal death. Ellicott explains that “the fact of what we call physical death is not denied, but… is regarded as the passage to a new and higher life” — “not through the infinite course of ages shall there be that which makes for him the sting of death” (cf. John 8:51). Barnes adds that the living believer has “the same prospects” as the pious dead, and “shall never come into eternal death” (John 6:50).

Calvin, for the Reformed, grounds the promise in the perpetuity of the life Christ gives: “he never permits the life which he has once bestowed to be lost.” Since “flesh is so frail, what would become of men, if, after having once obtained life, they were afterwards left to themselves?” The believer’s deathlessness rests not on his own strength but “on the power of Christ himself, that he may complete what he has begun,” for the soul, “born again of incorruptible seed” (1 Peter 1:23), has “Christ dwelling in it.” The witnesses then dwell on the searching question that follows — “Believest thou this?” Robertson calls it a “sudden test of Martha’s insight and faith,” and Clarke notes that God works for men “only in proportion to their faith.” The verse thus turns the great doctrine into a personal demand: having declared himself the resurrection and the life, Christ presses the truth home to the grieving sister, requiring not assent to a creed but trust in himself — the very faith that the next verse will draw from her lips.
27:
“She saith unto him, Yea, Lord: I believe that thou art the Christ, the Son of God, which should come into the world.” The witnesses hail this as a noble confession, the equal of Peter’s. The grammatical-historical commentators note its structure: the words “Yea, Lord” are her direct assent to his question, and what follows “is the foundation on which that answer rested” (Ellicott). Robertson dwells on the perfect tense “I have believed” (pepisteuka) — “my settled and firm faith,” the same tense Peter uses at John 6:69 — and judges that Martha’s confession “outranks those of Peter in Matthew 16:16, because she makes hers with her brother dead now four days,” and in the hope that Christ may yet raise him. Clarke notes that her faith, though it “acknowledges Christ for the Messiah promised to their fathers,” does not at this moment go further into the full meaning of his words, for, “having received some hope of her brother’s present resurrection, she waited for no farther instruction, but ran to call her sister.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, shows how the confession answers Christ’s claim. To prove that she believes what he has said of himself, Martha “replies that he is the Christ,” and “this knowledge includes the sum of all blessings,” for the Messiah was promised to “erect and prepare the true and perfect state of the kingdom of God.” By confessing him the Christ, the Son of God, she “strengthens her faith by the predictions of the prophets,” and so has ground to expect from him “the full restoration of all things.” The witnesses thus find in Martha’s words a faith that, whatever its earlier narrowness, now rises to lay hold of the Person: she may not grasp every depth of “I am the resurrection,” but she confesses the One in whom resurrection and life reside. Her great affirmation — made at the graveside of her brother — becomes the believing response that Christ’s self-disclosure sought, and the model of a faith that clings to Christ even in the dark.

The witnesses note that Martha’s confession, though true and great, does not yet take in all that Christ has said. She owns him as the Messiah, the Son of God, the long-expected One; but the full meaning of ‘I am the resurrection’ she will only learn at the grave itself. Her faith is real and rising, yet still on its way — enough to confess the Person, not yet enough to expect, without wavering, the wonder he is about to work.
28:
“And when she had so said, she went her way, and called Mary her sister secretly, saying, The Master is come, and calleth for thee.” The witnesses attend to the secrecy of the summons and the title given to Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that Martha called Mary “secretly… to avoid attracting the notice of the Jews who were with her” (Ellicott; v.19) — a care the sequel justifies (v.46). Barnes and Clarke note that the message implies Christ had asked for Mary: “the Master… calleth for thee,” “desiring her present, that he might strengthen her faith.” The title “the Master” (better “the Teacher,” Ellicott) “appears to have been the appellation by which he was known to the family” (Barnes), so that Mary “at once knows who is intended.”

The Eastern Chrysostom reads in Martha’s errand the calming power of Christ’s words: they “had the effect of stopping Martha’s grief,” so that “in her devotion to her Master she had no time to think of her afflictions.” Augustine notes she spoke “in a low voice,” and Bede observes that when Mary comes she will say less than Martha, for she “could not bring out what she wanted for weeping, as is usual with persons overwhelmed with sorrow.” The witnesses thus find the verse rich in small, true touches: the prudent secrecy that guards both the Lord and the coming miracle from the hostile mourners; the tender “calleth for thee,” showing that Christ sought out the sorrowing Mary by name; and the quiet evidence that his word to Martha had already begun its work, turning her from a mourner into a messenger who runs to bring her sister to the Lord of the resurrection.

The witnesses observe how naturally Mary is named at second hand. Martha, having met the Lord and confessed him, becomes herself the bearer of his call to her sister; the believer who has found Christ goes at once to bring another to him. And the message she carries is gentle and personal — not a summons to a crowd, but ‘the Master calleth for thee’ — the very form in which the soul most longs to hear itself sought by Christ in the hour of its grief.
29:
“As soon as she heard that, she arose quickly, and came unto him.” The witnesses dwell on the eager promptness of Mary’s response. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the haste: Robertson notes the aorist “arose quickly” (ēgerthē tachy), and Gill pictures her rising at once, without delay, the moment she hears that the Master calls. The contrast with the sitting posture of grief in which she had remained (v.20) is deliberate: the summons of Christ lifts her instantly from the stillness of mourning. Ellicott reads in the swiftness the measure of her love and reverence — she who had sat at his feet to hear his word (Luke 10:39) now springs up at his call, the same devotion that had kept her listening now hurrying her to his side.

The witnesses thus find in this brief verse a small but telling picture of the believer’s heart. Mary does not weigh the danger of the watching Jews, nor linger in her sorrow; the word “the Master calleth for thee” is enough, and she goes at once. Her promptness answers, on the side of love, what Martha’s confession answered on the side of faith: both sisters, each in her own way, are drawn out of grief toward the living Christ. The same eager obedience that makes her rise quickly will, at his feet, pour out the sorrow and the faith of her heart (v.32) — for the call of Christ, heard in the depths of mourning, raises the soul to seek him before it raises the dead from the grave.

The witnesses add that Mary’s haste is itself a kind of confession. Where words failed her for weeping, her feet spoke; the instant readiness to rise and come was the answer of a heart that, even in sorrow, had not let go of its Lord. In her swift obedience the church sees the proper response to every call of Christ — not delay, not calculation, but the eager rising of love to meet him wherever and whenever he summons.

The contrast with Martha is also instructive: the active sister came out before being called, while the contemplative Mary waits for the summons, and then comes with no less speed. Each sister moves toward Christ in her own way, but neither lingers once he is near. The verse thus rounds out the portrait of the household, and carries the narrative forward to the meeting at which Mary, falling at his feet, will draw from the Lord the tears that go before the raising of the dead.
30:
“Now Jesus was not yet come into the town, but was in that place where Martha met him.” The witnesses treat this brief parenthesis as a deliberate note explaining the scene that follows. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that Jesus had remained outside the village, near the burying-place: since “the Jewish burying places were without their cities and villages” (Clarke), “the place where our Saviour was… was not far from the place where Lazarus was buried.” Robertson reads the past-perfect parenthesis (as at v.19) and notes that Martha had her interview “while he was still coming” and then “went off… to hurry to Mary with the news.” Why Jesus tarried there, he grants, “we do not know,” but quotes Westcott’s suggestion: “as though He would meet the sisters away from the crowd of mourners.”

The Eastern Chrysostom, opening his homily here, turns aside to praise “the philosophy… which is with us,” the true wisdom that despises wealth and glory, against the heathen love of reputation — a reminder that the Lord who waits outside the town to comfort two grieving sisters embodies a greatness the world does not reckon. Calvin, for the Reformed, finds providence in the staying: though Martha had been sent home to draw Mary away from the assembly, “Christ had another design in view, which was, that the Jews might be witnesses of the miracle.” The witnesses thus read even this small geographical note as part of the careful staging of the sign: Christ halts short of the village, near the grave, both to meet the sisters apart in their grief and, by the very crowd that will follow, to gather the witnesses through whom the raising of Lazarus shall be made known beyond all doubt.

The witnesses note that the detail also explains the encounter to come. Because Jesus waited outside, near the grave rather than in the house of mourning, the meeting with Mary takes place in the open, before the following crowd — and so the tears of Christ, his question, and the raising itself are all done in the sight of many. What might have been a private comfort in the home becomes, by his deliberate waiting, a public sign; the place of his lingering is the place where the miracle’s witnesses are gathered.
31:
“The Jews then which were with her in the house, and comforted her, when they saw Mary, that she rose up hastily and went out, followed her, saying, She goeth unto the grave to weep there.” The witnesses note how the mourners’ kindness undoes Martha’s secrecy yet serves God’s purpose. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the Jews were engaged in “the prescribed ceremonial of those who were called comforters” (Ellicott), and, seeing Mary rise, “thinking she went to the grave to weep,” followed her. Clarke describes the custom of going “during the three days of weeping… to mourn near the graves,” for they supposed “the spirit hovered about the place… for three days.” Robertson observes that this crowd “meant kindly enough, but did the one wrong thing, for Mary wished to see Jesus alone” — “people with kind notions often so act” — so that “the secrecy of Martha was of no avail.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the frustrated secrecy as the hand of God. Martha had withdrawn Mary “from the numerous assembly,” yet “Christ had another design… that the Jews might be witnesses of the miracle.” They have no thought of it, “but it was no new thing that men should be led, as it were in darkness, and by the secret providence of God, where they did not intend to go.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a quiet instance of providence overruling human intentions: the comforters, meaning only to share Mary’s grief, unwittingly escort the very crowd that will see Lazarus raised. What Martha sought to keep private, and what the mourners did from mistaken kindness, alike serve the one end God intends — that the greatest of Christ’s signs should be done before many witnesses, and so carried, for belief or for hatred, to all Jerusalem.

The witnesses draw the lesson that God’s purposes are not thwarted by human plans, however well or ill conceived. Martha meant to keep the meeting quiet; the mourners meant only to share Mary’s grief; neither reckoned with the Lord’s intent. Yet the secrecy and the following alike bend to one end. So the verse becomes a small parable of providence, in which even the mistaken kindness of the crowd is woven into the design that will set the raising of Lazarus before the eyes of Jerusalem.
32:
“Then when Mary was come where Jesus was, and saw him, she fell down at his feet, saying unto him, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.” The witnesses mark Mary’s deeper prostration and her identical words. The grammatical-historical commentators note that she “fell down at his feet” — Robertson calls it an “impulsive act, like Mary” — and that her words are “precisely the same” as Martha’s (v.21), but “she said no more, only wept” (v.33). Ellicott suggests her emotion “was too powerful for words,” so that “the only possible language was that of a suppliant lying at His feet and weeping.” Wesley adds the touching contrast: “she fell at his feet — this Martha had not done. So she makes amends for her slowness in coming.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs both the reverence and its flaw. From Mary’s prostration “we learn that Christ was honoured in that house beyond the ordinary custom of men”; for though it was usual “to throw themselves down… in the presence of kings,” Christ “had nothing about him, according to the flesh, that was royal,” so that “she would not have done so, if she had not been convinced that he was the Son of God.” Yet Calvin marks the same fault as in Martha: “the power of Christ… ought not to have been limited” to his bodily presence, as though he could not have healed from afar. The witnesses thus find in Mary the portrait of a love deeper in feeling than in words — falling at his feet where Martha stood, weeping where Martha argued — and the same mixture of true faith with narrow expectation. Her grief, poured out at his feet, is the immediate occasion of the tears and the groaning of Christ that the next verse records.

The witnesses observe that Mary, who had once sat at Christ’s feet to learn (Lk 10:39), now falls at those same feet to weep. Her posture is the posture of worship, and her silence after the one broken sentence speaks more than Martha’s fuller words. In her the chapter shows a grief that does not argue but adores — casting itself down before the Lord and leaving its sorrow with him. It is this wordless weeping, more than any reasoning, that draws from Christ the tears and the deep groaning that follow.
33:
“When Jesus therefore saw her weeping, and the Jews also weeping which came with her, he groaned in the spirit, and was troubled.” The witnesses dwell on the mystery of Christ’s emotion — its reality, its cause, and its voluntariness. The grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with the strong verb “groaned” (enebrimēsato). Ellicott notes its “original meaning is to snort, as of horses,” passing to “disturbance of the mind, vehement agitation,” which may show itself “in sharp admonition, in words of anger… or in a physical shudder.” Barnes takes it here not of anger but of grief: “violent agitation… not from anger, but from grief,” for “he saw the sorrow of others, and was moved with sympathy and love.” Robertson observes that “Mary’s weeping was genuine,” while “that of the Jews was partly perfunctory and professional,” even “wailing” — “an incongruous combination,” which some take as a further cause of Christ’s indignation. Clarke draws the human comfort: “here the blessed Jesus shows himself to be truly man,” who “did not feel it beneath him to sympathize with the distressed, and weep with those who wept.”

The Latin Augustine and the Wesleyan Wesley press the voluntariness of the feeling. “Who but Himself could trouble Him?” asks Augustine: “Christ was troubled, because it pleased Him to be troubled… It was in His own power to be affected in this or that way or not,” for “the Word took up soul and flesh… and fitted it to Himself.” Wesley agrees the verb “he troubled himself” is “amazingly elegant”: Christ’s affections “were not properly passions, but voluntary emotions, which were wholly in his own power.” Chrysostom adds that he “humbled Himself, and let His human nature be seen, in order to gain them as witnesses.” Calvin, for the Reformed, finds the deepest cause beyond the immediate tears: while Christ truly “conforms to those mourners, so far as to weep along with them,” giving “proof that he has sympathy,” he “contemplated something higher, namely, the general misery of the whole human race” — the bondage of death over all. The witnesses thus hold together the two truths the verse joins: that Christ’s grief was real and tender, sharing our sorrow at the grave; and that it was no helpless passion but the free and ordered emotion of the Son, who feels our death the more deeply because he is its conqueror.
34:
“And said, Where have ye laid him? They said unto him, Lord, come and see.” The witnesses note the question of the omniscient Lord and its purposes. The grammatical-historical commentators agree that Christ asked “as man,” though “he, as the omniscient God, knew where he was laid” (Gill). Robertson compares it to John 6:6, where Jesus already “knew what he was going to do,” and calls it here “a simple question for information,” a polite request “for direction to the tomb.” Gill draws out the further ends: he asked so “that it might appear there was no juggle and contrivance” between him and the family, “to raise some expectation,” and “to draw the Jews thither, that they might be witnesses of the miracle.” Ellicott notes the brevity on both sides — “grief speaks in the fewest possible words” — and that the answer, “Lord, come and see,” is “the very language used by Philip to Nathanael” (John 1:46), a natural invitation by those who “had no idea of his purpose.”

Augustine (commenting from the previous verse) reads the question as a test and a parable: Christ “knew where he was, but He asked to try the faith of the people,” not wishing “to thrust the miracle upon them, but to make them ask for it, and thus do away with all suspicions.” He even hears in it an allusion to “our hidden calling,” the secret predestination by which we are called, Christ asking “as it were in ignorance, so long as we are ignorant ourselves.” Cyril of Jerusalem draws a different lesson from the scene — that “some are saved by others’ faith,” for the dead Lazarus could not believe for himself, but “what the dead man lacked was supplied by his true sisters.” The witnesses thus find the simple question rich with intention: the Lord who needs no direction asks the way to the grave, that the witnesses may lead him, expectation may rise, and the coming miracle may be set beyond all suspicion of collusion — drawing the very mourners to the tomb where their grief will be turned to wonder.
35:
“Jesus wept.” The witnesses make much of the shortest verse in the Bible, which Barnes calls “exceedingly important and tender.” The grammatical-historical commentators stress that it “shows the Lord Jesus as a friend, a tender friend, and evinces his character as a man” (Barnes); Gill adds that it proves him “truly and really man, subject to like passions, only without sin.” Robertson marks that the word here (edakrysen, “shed tears,” used only here in the New Testament) differs from the “weeping” (klaiō) of Mary and the Jews: Christ’s were silent tears, not loud wailing — a quiet, real grief. Gill suggests the springs of those tears: he wept “meditating upon the state of his friend Lazarus, the distress his two sisters were in,” and even “the greater damnation that would befall the Jews… who, notwithstanding the miracle, would not believe in him.”

The commentators draw the pastoral lessons the verse has always taught the church. Barnes: “the most tender personal friendship is not inconsistent with the most pure religion”; “piety binds stronger the ties of friendship,” and “it is right, it is natural, it is indispensable for the Christian to sympathize with others in their afflictions” (Romans 12:15). Clarke rebukes those who would call tears at the grave of the dead a weakness or a sin: “he who says so… is a reproach to the name of man,” for “such apathy never came from God.” The witnesses thus find in these two words the fullest proof of Christ’s true and tender humanity. He who is the resurrection and the life, who knows he is about to call Lazarus from the tomb, does not despise the sorrow of the grave but enters it, weeping with those who weep — so that the same Lord who will conquer death also hallows the tears of those who mourn, and shows that to grieve in love is no denial of faith but the mark of a heart like his own.

The witnesses also note the wonder that the Lord of the resurrection should weep at all. He stands moments from calling Lazarus alive, and knows it; yet he does not hold himself aloof from the grief around him, but shares it to the point of tears. In this the church has always found its deepest comfort: that the One who conquers death first weeps beside the grave, entering our sorrow before he ends it, so that no mourner need feel that faith forbids tears, nor that the risen life Christ gives is cold to the losses of the present.
36:
“Then said the Jews, Behold how he loved him!” The witnesses note the verdict of the onlookers and divide them, with Ellicott, into two parties (the second in v.37). The grammatical-historical commentators read this first remark as candid and even admiring: the Jews “supposed that these tears were shed purely on his account,” and judged that they “proceeded from an hearty and sincere affection” (Gill). Ellicott and Robertson note the strong word for love (ephilei, the imperfect, “how he used to love him,” the same word the sisters used in v.3): “how He must have loved him during his life,” they reason, “if He thus sheds tears for him after his death!” Clarke turns the exclamation upon the gospel: when we see Christ “pouring out his blood and life upon the cross for mankind, we may with exultation and joy cry out, Behold how he hath loved us!”

Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs the two opinions the Evangelist records. Those who say “behold how he loved him,” though “they think less highly of Christ than they ought,” ascribing to him “nothing but what may belong to a man,” yet “speak of him with greater candour and modesty” than the cavillers who follow. The witnesses thus find in this verse the gentler of the two responses to Christ’s tears: the crowd, moved by so visible a grief, owns the reality of his love even while missing his glory. Their words, true as far as they go, stand as the church’s own perpetual wonder before the weeping Christ — that the Lord of life should so love a mortal man as to weep at his grave; and they prepare, by contrast, for the colder voices that turn the very same tears into a cavil against his power.

The witnesses observe that even this admiring verdict falls short of the truth.
