The Jews see in Christ’s tears the love of a friend for a friend, and so far they speak rightly; but they do not yet suspect the love of the Lord of life for one he is about to raise. Their wonder is real but small — measuring his affection by the grief of an ordinary man — and it cannot reach the deeper love that weeps at the grave precisely because it means to conquer it. The verse thus marks how near, and how far, mere admiration of Christ may stand from true faith in him.
37:
“And some of them said, Could not this man, which opened the eyes of the blind, have caused that even this man should not have died?” The witnesses hear in this second party a cavil that turns Christ’s tears against him. The grammatical-historical commentators identify them as those “averse to him… willing to put the worst construction on every action” (Gill). Clarke paraphrases their malice: “if he loved him so well, why did he not heal him? … Let none hereafter vaunt the miracle of the blind man’s cure.” Ellicott notes they appeal to “the greatest miracle… within the limits of their own knowledge” — the opening of the blind man’s eyes (chapter 9) — passing over the raisings of the dead “they had not believed”; their question is meant “to imply that He could not have prevented” the death. Robertson observes the imperfect “could not” (ouk edynato) rather than the present “can,” and that the cure of chapter 9, done some three months before, “had made a lasting impression.” Wesley marks “a strange mixture of faith and unbelief”: they grant the miracle on the blind man, “yet they never dreamed that he could raise him again.”

The witnesses thus set the two parties side by side: the one moved to confess his love, the other to question his power. The cavillers’ words are subtler than open mockery — they do not deny the earlier miracle, but use it to insinuate either that his love was weak or his power limited, since (they assume) he would surely have spared his friend had he been able. Their half-faith makes their unbelief the more culpable: confessing what he did for a stranger’s eyes, they will not believe what he is about to do at a friend’s grave. Cyril of Jerusalem, citing the prophecy that the Messiah would open blind eyes and raise the dead (Isaiah 35:5), points past their doubt to the very sign Christ is about to give — the answer their question did not expect.

The witnesses note how the same tears yield opposite verdicts. To the one party they prove his love; to the other they betray a supposed weakness. So the works of Christ ever divide his hearers according to the disposition of their hearts: what softens the believing hardens the captious. The cavillers, fixing on the blind man’s cure, reason from his power to his blame — if he could heal, why did he not? — and in so reasoning shut their eyes to the greater wonder at hand, by which their very question would be answered beyond all reply.
38:
“Jesus therefore again groaning in himself cometh to the grave. It was a cave, and a stone lay upon it.” The witnesses note the renewed emotion and the setting of the tomb, and debate the cause of the groaning. The grammatical-historical commentators link it directly to the cavil just heard: Ellicott says “their evil thoughts, expressed in v.37, are the cause of this new emotion of anger,” and Gill reads it as “an holy anger and indignation at the malice and wickedness of the Jews” joined to grief. Robertson marks the same verb as in v.33 (embrimōmenos), now “in himself” rather than “in his spirit,” for “the speculation concerning his power stirred the depths of his nature again.” On the tomb, the commentators describe the common Jewish sepulchre “dug in the rock,” horizontally or vertically, the mouth closed “with a great stone” (Clarke, Ellicott, Wesley, who recalls the cave of Machpelah, Genesis 49:29–31).

Calvin, for the Reformed, offers a deeper reading of the groan. Christ “does not approach the sepulchre as an idle spectator, but as a champion who prepares for a contest”; therefore “we need not wonder that he groans, for the violent tyranny of death, which he had to conquer, is placed before his eyes.” He grants that some refer the groan to indignation at unbelief, but judges it “far more appropriate” that “he contemplated the transaction itself rather than the men” — that is, the horror of death he came to overthrow. The witnesses thus hold both readings together: Christ is moved both by holy indignation at the hardness that cavils even at the grave, and by the deeper agitation of one who, advancing to the tomb, faces the last enemy he has come to vanquish. The groaning is no mere passion but the bracing of the Conqueror as he comes to do battle with death itself.

The witnesses thus see Christ approach the tomb in a frame far from cold detachment. He comes groaning, stirred both by the unbelief that surrounds him and by the dread reality of death he is about to confront. The careful note of the cave and the sealing stone fixes the scene of the contest: here, behind a stone, lies the four-days corruption that is the trophy of the last enemy. It is to this that the Lord of life advances, not as a curious onlooker but as the Champion who will, in a moment, despoil the grave of its prey.
39:
“Jesus said, Take ye away the stone. Martha, the sister of him that was dead, saith unto him, Lord, by this time he stinketh: for he hath been dead four days.” The witnesses dwell on the command, the proof of death, and Martha’s relapse into doubt. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Christ ordered the stone removed not because he needed it — “he that could command him to come forth, could have commanded away the stone” — but “that the corpse might be seen, and even smelt,” so “that it might be manifest there was no fallacy” or collusion (Gill, Clarke). Barnes presses the apologetic force of the detail: the four days and the corruption “prove that there could be no deception… no suspended animation,” and such “minute circumstances” are “marks of truth,” for “impostors deal in generals only.” Robertson renders tetartaios literally — “he is a fourth-day man” — and Lightfoot notes the three days of weeping were past and the days of lamentation begun, “so that all hope… was wholly gone.”

On Martha’s words the commentators are gently critical. Ellicott catches the pathos in the phrase “the sister of him that was dead”: it is the sister in her that speaks, unable to bear that “the form of him she loved has now passed to corruption.” But Calvin, for the Reformed, names the fault: her objection “is an indication of distrust, for she promises herself less from the power of Christ than she ought.” The root of it is “measuring the infinite and incomprehensible power of God by the perception of her flesh” — since nothing seems more contrary to life than “putrefaction and offensive smell,” she infers “no remedy can be found.” Wesley sums the moment: “thus did reason and faith struggle together.” The witnesses thus find in this verse both the careful establishing of an undeniable death and a last flicker of the limited faith Christ has been patiently enlarging — the same Martha who confessed him the Christ now stumbling at the stench of the grave, on the very threshold of the glory he had promised her she would see.
40:
“Jesus saith unto her, Said I not unto thee, that, if thou wouldest believe, thou shouldest see the glory of God?” The witnesses read this as Christ’s gentle reproof of Martha’s doubt and his recall of his promise. The grammatical-historical commentators note that these exact words are not recorded earlier, but their substance was implied in what he had said about the resurrection and his own person (vv.23–26); Gill, Robertson, and Calvin agree that “something more was said to Martha than John has literally related,” or that this is the plain sense of “Believest thou this?” (v.26) and of the message in v.4. Ellicott observes that Christ “takes her back to the promise… which had led to her brightest hopes,” and gently marks the decline: “her last remark had more of the human and less of the divine than was contained in her earliest words” (v.22). Clarke draws the doctrine: “it is faith alone that interests the miraculous and saving power of God in behalf of men.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, unfolds the relation of faith and sight that the verse teaches. Christ “reproves Martha’s distrust, in not forming a hope sufficiently vigorous from the promise.” And he draws the principle: faith is required “not only because faith opens our eyes, that we may be able to see the power of God shining in his works, but because our faith prepares the way for the power, mercy, and goodness of God.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the condition on which the coming miracle is hung: not that faith earns the resurrection, but that faith is the open eye and the open hand through which the glory of God is seen and received. Martha is bidden to believe, that she may behold; and the order — first believing, then seeing — answers and corrects the world’s demand to see before it will believe. On the threshold of the grave, Christ asks of the doubting sister the one thing he has asked throughout: that she trust him, and so witness the glory of God.
41:
“Then they took away the stone… And Jesus lifted up his eyes, and said, Father, I thank thee that thou hast heard me.” The witnesses divide over the nature of this prayer — whether it bespeaks the Son’s dependence or his sovereignty — and agree on its purpose. The grammatical-historical commentators note the attitude and the thanksgiving: the lifted eyes are “an attitude of prayer” (Barnes), and the thanks are offered, Clarke says, because, since men held that miracles might be wrought by evil power, Jesus “invoked the supreme God before these unbelieving Jews, that they might see that it was by his power” alone. Robertson stresses that “clearly Jesus had prayed to the Father concerning the raising of Lazarus” and “has the answer before he acts” — quoting Dods, “no pomp of incantation, no wrestling in prayer even; but simple words of thanksgiving, as if already Lazarus was restored.” Wesley, however, denies any petition for aid: Jesus “wrought the miracle with an air of absolute sovereignty, as the Lord of life and death,” thanking the Father only that providence had granted “this remarkable opportunity of exerting my power.” Alcuin, by contrast, reads it of his humanity: “Christ, as man, being inferior to the Father, prays to Him for Lazarus’s resurrection.”

The fathers add their characteristic depths. Origen bids us pray “after Christ’s pattern,” lifting “the eyes of our heart… above present things.” Chrysostom finds in the lowly form of the words Christ’s care “not how He may utter some sublime saying, but how something able to draw us to Him,” accommodating himself to weak hearers. Gregory and Augustine read the three raisings of the Gospels as three depths of sin — the maiden dead in the house (sin in the heart), the young man borne out the gate (sin in the act), and Lazarus buried under a stone (sin become habit) — “but the Divine grace has regard even to such.” Calvin, for the Reformed, gathers the sense: the lifted eyes are “the token of a mind truly prepared for prayer,” yet the thanksgiving shows that Christ stands in such favour with the Father that he is heard always (v.42). The witnesses thus find the prayer to be at once true prayer and no mark of weakness: the Son, one in will with the Father, gives thanks aloud not because he doubts the issue but that the watching crowd may know the work is from God.
42:
“And I knew that thou hearest me always: but because of the people which stand by I said it, that they may believe that thou hast sent me.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s own explanation of the audible prayer — that it was spoken for the bystanders, not from any need of his own. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the emphatic pronoun: “I, for My part, knew” (Ellicott), “it is not for My own sake that I speak these words.” Robertson reads the past perfect “I knew” of a settled, lifelong certainty — “it has always (pantote) been so” — and the purpose clause “that they may come to believe that thou didst send me.” Barnes adds that “Jesus never prayed in vain… and in all his miracles the ground of his joy was, not that he was to be praised, but that others were to be benefited and God glorified.” Ellicott grounds the “hearing always” in the unbroken union of wills: “every prayer of the Son was an expression of the will of the Father… not exceptional, but the law of His human life,” with the ever-present consciousness, “I and my Father are one” (John 10:30).

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the verse as an “anticipation, lest any one should think that he did not stand so high in favour with the Father.” Christ means “that there is so great an agreement between him and the Father, that the Father refuses him nothing; and even that he had no need to pray, because he only executed what he knew that the Father had enjoined” — yet he “called on the name of the Father,” that men “may be more fully assured that this is truly a divine work.” The witnesses thus find here the key to the whole prayer: the spoken thanksgiving was an act of condescension to the crowd, a public attestation that the raising about to be wrought is the work of God owning his Son. Christ does not pray as one uncertain of being heard, but speaks aloud so that the witnesses, seeing the answer, may believe that he is sent of the Father — turning even his prayer into a sign.
43:
“And when he had thus spoken, he cried with a loud voice, Lazarus, come forth.” The witnesses dwell on the loud cry, the naming, and the omnipotent word. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the “loud voice” as a deliberate assertion of power, the opposite of magic: “the ancient magicians and jugglers performed their wonders by whispering and muttering” (Barnes; cf. Isaiah 8:19), but Jesus “spake openly and audibly,” the cry being “not for the benefit of Lazarus, but for the sake of the crowd… that they might see that Lazarus came forth simultaneously with the command” (Robertson). Clarke sees here the fulfilment of Christ’s own word that “the hour is coming… when the dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God, and live” (John 5:25): he “cries aloud, that the people may… see that even death is subject to the sovereign command of Christ.” Ellicott notes that the cry marks the moment “life returned,” and that Jesus addresses Lazarus by name, “as we should address a friend whom we wished to arouse from sleep” (compare Mary at the tomb, John 20:16).

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the theology of the word. By raising Lazarus “not touching with the hand, but only” speaking, “his Divine power is more fully demonstrated,” and he “holds out to our view the secret and astonishing efficacy of his word. For how did Christ restore life to the dead but by the word?” In this, Calvin adds, “he exhibited a visible token of his spiritual grace, which we experience every day… when he shows that his voice gives life.” The witnesses thus find in the brief command the very nerve of the miracle and of the gospel: the dead man is raised by nothing but the spoken word of Christ, the same word by which sinners dead in trespasses are quickened, and by which, at the last day, all that are in the graves shall hear his voice and come forth. “Lazarus, come forth” is the audible proof that the Son of God has only to speak, and death obeys.
44:
“And he that was dead came forth, bound hand and foot with graveclothes: and his face was bound about with a napkin. Jesus saith unto them, Loose him, and let him go.” The witnesses note a wonder within the wonder, and the command that follows. The grammatical-historical commentators describe the burial wrappings — the body “swathed or bound” in linen bands (keiriai), the face covered with a napkin (soudarion) — and Lightfoot points to “a second miracle in this great miracle”: “the dead man came forth, though bound hand and foot with his graveclothes, and blinded with the napkin,” so that his walking out so bound was itself beyond nature. Gill marks that “he who had been dead, being now made alive… came out of the cave.” The reality and completeness of the raising — the same man, “body and soul” (Barnes), now living and moving — is set beyond dispute.

On the closing command, “Loose him, and let him go,” the witnesses observe Christ’s way of giving to others what they can do, while reserving to himself what only he can. He who alone could call the dead to life now bids the bystanders unbind the grave-clothes — much as he had bidden them take away the stone (v.39) — so that they, handling the living man, might be the surer witnesses that he was truly raised. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the scene a picture of the gospel’s effect: Christ raises the dead soul by his word, and then would have it loosed from the grave-clothes of its old bondage and set free to walk. The witnesses thus close the miracle on a note both literal and figurative: Lazarus, called from the tomb and freed from its wrappings, stands a living proof that the voice of Christ is the resurrection and the life — and a pledge of the day when all his own shall come forth, loosed forever from the bonds of death.

The witnesses note too that Christ’s command spares the bystanders nothing they are able to do, while reserving to himself what only omnipotence can. To call a four-days corpse to life is his alone; to unwind the linen bands is theirs. By so dividing the work he both honours their part and binds them as handlers, not merely watchers, of the risen man — a detail that, like the rolling away of the stone, leaves the miracle without any possible charge of illusion. Lazarus restored, freed, and given back to his sisters is the crowning sign before the Passion, and the unanswerable answer to every cavil heard at the grave.
45:
“Then many of the Jews which came to Mary, and had seen the things which Jesus did, believed on him.” The witnesses mark the first and happiest fruit of the miracle. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that these believers were eye-witnesses: Robertson notes they “beheld” (theasamenoi) the deed and “did not depend on hearsay,” so that the miracle “carries conviction to every heart, and leaves no further possibility of doubt” (Ellicott). Clarke observes that their very errand of kindness became their salvation — “their friendly visit to these distressed sisters became the means of their conversion” — and applies the proverb, “it is better to go to the house of mourning than to the house of feasting” (Ecclesiastes 7:2). Henry, for the Puritans, reads it as the usual double effect of Christ’s works: “to some it was a savour of life unto life, to others of death unto death,” and here many “were induced to believe,” the miracle being “an incontestable proof of his divine mission.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, distinguishes the kinds of faith a miracle produces. “Miracles have a twofold use: they are intended either to prepare us for faith, or to confirm us in faith.” Here the former is meant — the sign drew these Jews to “regard Christ with admiration and reverence, so as to submit to be his disciples” — yet he adds that “the bare miracle could not have been sufficient to produce faith” without the inward work of the word and Spirit. Wesley notes simply that in their believing “the Son of God was glorified, according to what our Lord had said” (v.4). The witnesses thus find in this verse the harvest the whole chapter was ordered to yield: the raising of Lazarus, done before many witnesses, becomes the occasion of faith for many, fulfilling the purpose Christ had named — that the sickness was “for the glory of God,” that the Son might be glorified, and that, beholding, they might believe.

The witnesses note that this believing remnant vindicates the whole costly delay. The two days that left the sisters in their grief, the four days in the tomb, the tears and the groaning — all bore fruit when many, beholding Lazarus alive, gave their hearts to Christ. What had seemed to the household an unbearable slowness proved the very means of a wider faith, so that the loss of one brother for a season became the gaining of many souls forever.
46:
“But some of them went their ways to the Pharisees, and told them what things Jesus had done.” The witnesses note the opposite effect of the same sign and debate the disposition of these reporters. The grammatical-historical commentators marvel at the hardening: Barnes observes “the different effect which the word and works of God will have on different individuals… the evidence of this miracle was as clear to the one as the other,” yet these “would not be convinced.” Clarke is more severe — “some that had seen even this miracle steeled their hearts against it, and… conspired the destruction of this most humane, amiable, and glorious Saviour.” But Ellicott resists making them simple informers: it is “contrary to their position as believers to think that they did this as informers against Jesus”; rather, what they had seen “carried conviction to their own minds, and they report it to the Pharisees… as a proof that He really was the Messiah, or… to demand from them a judgment on the facts.” Robertson reads them as those “deeply impressed” yet lacking “the courage to break away from the rabbis without consulting them.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, takes the darker view: in these he sees “detestable ingratitude, or rather horrible rage,” for “the resurrection of Lazarus ought… to have softened even hearts of stone,” yet “there is no work of God which impiety will not infect and corrupt.” The lesson he draws is that “before men can profit by miracles, their hearts must be purified.” Wesley hears in the verse “a dreadful confirmation” of the parable’s word, “if they hear not Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded though one rose from the dead” (Luke 16:31). The witnesses thus find the same raising dividing its witnesses to the end: to some a savour of life, to others the spur to betrayal. Whether the reporters went as half-believers seeking a verdict or as enemies bearing information, the result is the same — the greatest of Christ’s signs becomes the immediate occasion of the council that will plot his death.
47:
“Then gathered the chief priests and the Pharisees a council, and said, What do we? for this man doeth many miracles.” The witnesses are struck that the rulers should answer so great a sign with a plot. Theophylact: “such a miracle… should have drawn forth wonder and praise. But they make it a reason of plotting against His life.” Augustine notes “they had no thought of believing,” but “only consulted how they might hurt and kill Him, not how themselves might be saved from death.” Chrysostom marvels that, with “such certain proofs” of his divinity before them, “they call only a man.” The grammatical-historical commentators describe the body: the Sanhedrin (Robertson notes its only naming in John), in which “both chief priests (Sadducees) and Pharisees combine,” the Sadducean priests now taking the lead. Barnes presses the damning admission in their own question: “if they admitted that he performed miracles, it was clear what they ought to do — they should have received him as the Messiah,” yet they ask only “what measures are we taking to arrest the progress of his sentiments?”

Calvin, for the Reformed, names the “monstrous blindness” of the scene: had they not been “stupid and brutish, they would at least have been impressed with some reverence for Christ,” yet “they now assemble deliberately… to bury the glory of God.” They do not say they make war on God, but “as they cannot extinguish Christ but by overturning the power of God,” that is what they do. Henry, for the Puritans, finds here “a clear discovery of the madness… in man’s heart, and of its desperate enmity against God.” The witnesses thus read the council as the tragic climax of unbelief: confronted with a miracle they cannot deny — their own question concedes that “this man doeth many miracles” — the rulers neither believe nor doubt the fact, but resolve to destroy the One who works it. The undeniable sign, which made many believe, drives the hardened to murder.

The witnesses observe the strange paralysis of the council: they neither deny the miracle nor draw the only honest conclusion from it. Their words betray them — to admit ‘many miracles’ and yet ask ‘what do we?’ is to confess the truth and resolve to fight it. So unbelief is shown to be not a failure of evidence but a hardness of will; the rulers see, and because they see, they plot. The very clarity of the sign, which converted the simple, only sharpened the malice of the proud.
48:
“If we let him thus alone, all men will believe on him: and the Romans shall come and take away both our place and nation.” The witnesses expose the political calculation and its self-condemning irony. The grammatical-historical commentators read the fear plainly: another such sign, especially in the city, would carry conviction “to the minds of all” (Ellicott), and, supposing Jesus aimed at a temporal kingdom, the rulers dreaded that Rome “would be so irritated” as to “destroy our temple, and utterly dissolve our civil and ecclesiastical existence” (Clarke). Robertson parses the conditional — “suppose we leave him thus alone” and “suppose he keeps on raising the dead right here next door to Jerusalem” — and the inevitable conclusion, “all (pantes) will believe,” not merely some, and “the Romans will come.” Barnes notes the “place” is the temple and the city, held only “at the will of the Roman emperor.” Lightfoot suspects the council half-knew the truth, comparing the vine-dressers’ words, “this is the heir; come, let us kill him” (Mark 12:7) — knowing he was the heir, yet resolving on his death “that the inheritance might be ours.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the guilt beneath the statecraft: they are “fully convinced that it lies in their power to block up Christ’s path,” and so, unable to extinguish him “but by overturning the power of God,” they set themselves against God under the pretext of the public good. The witnesses do not fail to mark the tragic irony the verse contains: the very catastrophe the council schemes to prevent — that “the Romans shall come and take away both our place and nation” — they bring upon themselves by rejecting and killing the Messiah, when Rome did indeed come, within a generation, and destroy the temple and the nation. Seeking to save their place by his death, they lost it; the murder meant to preserve them sealed their ruin.

The witnesses also note how the council prefers a calculation of self-interest to the question of truth. Not once do they ask whether Jesus is the Christ; they ask only how his growing influence will affect their place and power. This is the root of their sin — that the love of position blinds them to the visitation of God. And the irony stands as a warning for every age: those who reject Christ to preserve their worldly stake most surely lose it, for the kingdom they defend against him is the kingdom they thereby forfeit.
49:
“And one of them, named Caiaphas, being the high priest that same year, said unto them, Ye know nothing at all.” The witnesses note the contemptuous statecraft of the high priest and the hidden working of the Spirit. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the phrase “high priest that same year”: under the Romans the office, once for life, had become almost annual, passed about among a few families (Gill, Robertson), so John marks the fateful year of the crucifixion. Caiaphas brushes aside the council’s perplexity with “ye know nothing at all,” the brusque scorn of a hardened politician cutting through their hesitation to propose the simplest solution — the death of one man.

The fathers fasten on the strange fact that a wicked man should here prophesy. Chrysostom marvels at “the great virtue of the Holy Spirit, in drawing forth a prophecy from a wicked man,” and at “the virtue of the pontifical office, which made him, though an unworthy High Priest, unconsciously prophesy” — for “Divine grace only used his mouth; it touched not his corrupt heart.” Origen qualifies it carefully: “not everyone that prophesies is a prophet,” as “not everyone that does a just action is just”; Caiaphas “prophesied without being a prophet, as did Balaam,” his intention being “not to induce his hearers to believe… but to excite them to kill Him.” The witnesses thus find in Caiaphas a sobering wonder: the office, not the man, gives weight to his words, so that the very high priest who plots the death of Christ becomes, against his will and his meaning, the mouthpiece of a prophecy of that death’s saving purpose — which the next verses will unfold.

The witnesses add that John’s note ‘that same year’ is heavy with meaning: it was the year of all years, the year in which the true High Priest would offer himself. That the official high priest of that very year should, in plotting the death of Christ, pronounce its saving purpose is among the deepest ironies of the Gospel. The man means murder; the office, overruled by God, speaks atonement — a sign that even the malice of Christ’s enemies is bent, unwilling, to serve the counsel of God.
50:
“Nor consider that it is expedient for us, that one man should die for the people, and that the whole nation perish not.” The witnesses read Caiaphas’s maxim on two levels — the cynical statecraft he intends, and the saving truth the Spirit speaks through him. On the surface, the grammatical-historical commentators agree, it is cold political calculation: better to sacrifice one man, however innocent, than to risk the destruction of the nation by Rome (Gill, Barnes). Robertson notes the words “expedient” (sympherei) and “die for the people” (hyper), and the ruthless logic by which Caiaphas “solved” the council’s difficulty. Ellicott catches the high priest’s contempt: the others have scruples and hesitations; he sweeps them aside with a brutal calculus of raison d’état, urging the judicial murder of Jesus as the lesser evil.

Yet the witnesses, with John, hear beneath the maxim a truth deeper than its speaker knew. Caiaphas meant only that it was politically convenient for Jesus to die; but the words he uttered are, in their true sense, the very heart of the gospel — that one man should indeed die for the people, that the nation, and the world, “perish not.” The high priest, whose office it was to offer sacrifice, unwittingly pronounces the saving necessity of the one great Sacrifice, substituting himself for the many. Calvin, for the Reformed, marks how God overrules the wicked tongue to speak his counsel. The witnesses thus find the verse poised between its two meanings, which the next verse (v.51) will expressly divide: Caiaphas the politician proposing a judicial murder, and Caiaphas the high priest, despite himself, prophesying the atonement — the death of one for all that is the church’s only hope.

The witnesses draw the lesson that God can make even an enemy’s scheme the vehicle of his truth. Caiaphas reaches for a maxim of expediency to justify a judicial murder, and in the same breath utters the profoundest doctrine of the faith — that one should die for the many. The verse thus stands as a marvel of providence: the very words framed to destroy Christ become, rightly heard, the announcement of the salvation his death would accomplish. What the high priest meant as policy, the Spirit meant as prophecy.
51:
“And this spake he not of himself: but being high priest that year, he prophesied that Jesus should die for that nation.” The witnesses follow John in reading Caiaphas’s maxim as an unconscious prophecy. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that the high priest meant only his own cruel design, yet God overruled his tongue. Barnes: “though he uttered what proved to be a true prophecy, yet it was accomplished in a way which he did not intend… God so ordered it that he delivered a most precious truth respecting the atonement.” Robertson notes “not wholly of himself” — “there was more in what Caiaphas said than he understood” — and calls it “unconscious prophecy… purely accidental,” for “Caiaphas meant only what was mean and selfish.” Ellicott finds “a moral beauty in the words, in spite of the diabolical intent,” and likens him to “another Balaam,” “the oracle of God in spite of himself.” Lightfoot resists ascribing the gift to the office — “Is Caiaphas among the prophets?” — but John’s own phrase, “being high priest that year,” the others take as marking why God spoke through him at that fateful hour.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the careful distinction. The Evangelist “does not mean that Caiaphas, like one out of his senses, uttered what he did not understand,” for “he spoke what was his own opinion”; rather, “a higher impulse guided his tongue, because God intended that he should make known… something higher than what occurred to his mind.” So Caiaphas “might be said to have two tongues” — one vomiting “the wicked and cruel design of putting Christ to death,” the other, against his will, proclaiming the saving purpose of that death. The witnesses thus find the verse a striking instance of God’s sovereignty over the words of the wicked: the very high priest who plots the murder pronounces, in the same breath, its true meaning — that Jesus should die “for the nation,” the one for the many, the heart of the atonement uttered by its enemy.

The witnesses draw the comfort that God is sovereign even over the speech of his enemies. The high priest who pronounced the death-sentence pronounced also its meaning, so that the church reads the doctrine of the atonement in the mouth of the man who plotted the crucifixion. What was framed as the cold policy of a council becomes, in the providence of God, the first announcement of why Christ must die — that one should perish for the people, and the people not perish.
52:
“And not for that nation only, but that also he should gather together in one the children of God that were scattered abroad.” The witnesses take John’s own gloss as opening the prophecy to its full, universal scope. The grammatical-historical commentators note the widening of terms: Caiaphas had said Jesus should die “for the people” (the Jews), but John says “for that nation,” and now passes to “the children of God” of every land (Ellicott). Gill explains that, “though Christ as prophet was sent to the Jews only,” yet “as a priest he did not die for them only, but for the Gentiles also, even for the whole mystical Israel of God.” Clarke and Barnes read it as “one Church out of the Jewish and Gentile believers,” the gathering of the “other sheep” of John 10:16 into “one flock under one shepherd” (cf. 1 John 2:2; Ephesians 2:14–17). Robertson notes the perfect participle “that are scattered abroad” (diaskorpismena), the children of God dispersed through the nations, whom the death of Christ will gather.

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the doctrine of election in the phrase. The reconciliation Christ effects “is extended to the Gentiles”; but how are those called “children of God” who, “in consequence of being wretchedly scattered and wandering, became the enemies of God”? The answer is that “God had in his breast” those who “in themselves were wandering and lost sheep, or rather… wolves and wild beasts” — “it is therefore by election that he reckons as children… even before they are called,” those who are yet far off. Wesley sums the scope: Christ died to “gather into one Church all the children of God that were scattered abroad through all ages and nations.” The witnesses thus find in this verse the gospel’s widest horizon, drawn out of a murderer’s maxim: the death of the one man is for a people beyond all peoples — the ingathering of God’s scattered children, Jew and Gentile, into a single flock by the cross.

The witnesses note how John lifts the saying from a national to a cosmic scope. The death that Caiaphas weighed as a means of saving the Jewish state is, in truth, the means of saving a people gathered out of every nation. The cross does not merely spare Israel from Rome; it gathers into one the scattered children of God from the ends of the earth. So the narrowest of motives is made to utter the widest of mercies, and the murder of one man becomes the ingathering of the world.
53:
“Then from that day forth they took counsel together for to put him to death.” The witnesses mark this as the official, settled resolve of the Sanhedrin. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Caiaphas’s advice carried the council: his “judgment silenced opposition” (Barnes), and they were now “of one accord in the business” (Clarke), waiting only “for a proper opportunity.” Ellicott states it plainly: “on that day the Sanhedrin officially decreed His death,” the only remaining question being “how they could carry out this decree without exciting a popular tumult, or bringing themselves into collision with the Romans” (cf. Matthew 26:4). Robertson observes that “the raising of Lazarus brought matters to a head,” the end now “not more than a month” away, at the Passover. Gill adds that the decree was assented to generally, “except by one or two, as Nicodemus, and Joseph of Arimathea.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that the council had “already determined to put him to death; it only remained to advise in what way they could carry their resolution into effect.” The witnesses thus find in this verse the grim turning of the chapter: the sign that gave life to Lazarus and faith to many becomes, for the rulers, the trigger of a death-warrant. The greatest demonstration of Christ’s power over death hardens his enemies into a fixed purpose to kill him — a purpose that, by the deepest irony of the Gospel, will accomplish the very atonement Caiaphas had unwittingly foretold. From this day the road runs straight to the cross; and the One who raised the dead walks knowingly toward the death by which he will gather the scattered children of God.

The witnesses observe the dark symmetry of the chapter. It began with a death that Christ would turn to life and glory; it ends with a council resolving on a death meant to extinguish him. Yet the very decree that aims to destroy him will, in God’s hand, accomplish the redemption Caiaphas had foretold. The rulers think to end his work; they are in fact setting in motion its consummation, for the death they plot is the death by which the world is saved.
54:
“Jesus therefore walked no more openly among the Jews; but went thence unto a country near to the wilderness, into a city called Ephraim, and there continued with his disciples.” The witnesses read the withdrawal as prudence, not fear, and as an example. The Latin Augustine insists it was “not that His power had failed Him; for, had He pleased, He might still have walked openly among the Jews, and they done nothing to Him,” but “He wished to show the disciples, by His own example, that believers did not sin by retiring from danger.” The Eastern Chrysostom notes how it “must have troubled the disciples to see Him save Himself by merely human means,” yet they “continued with Him” (Luke 22:28). The grammatical-historical commentators agree: “Jesus never exposed his life unnecessarily to hazard,” for “although the time of his death was determined… this did not prevent his using proper means to preserve his life” (Barnes); Robertson notes that to remain “would bring on the end now instead of his hour,” which was “at the passover a month ahead,” and that he withdrew to the thinly-peopled hill-country, to Ephraim near Bethel (cf. 2 Chronicles 13:19).

Calvin, for the Reformed, guards the motive: Christ “did not fly in order to withdraw from his Father’s calling; for he had no other intention than to present himself to undergo voluntary death at the time which God had appointed.” The withdrawal is thus the free act of one ordering his own hour, not the flight of a man overtaken by events. Origen reads it mystically — the Word, long coming openly to the Jews through the prophets, now “went from there” — and Henry, for the Puritans, finds the comfort that “no devices of man can alter the purposes of God,” for “Jesus still orders all things for his own glory and the salvation of his people.” The witnesses thus find in the retirement to Ephraim a deliberate pause before the end: Christ, master of his hour, steps aside from his enemies’ reach until the Passover when, of his own will, he will return to die.
55:
“And the Jews’ passover was nigh at hand: and many went out of the country up to Jerusalem before the passover, to purify themselves.” The witnesses note the approach of the feast and the customary preparations. The grammatical-historical commentators identify this as “the fourth passover from Christ’s entrance on his public ministry, and the last he ate with his disciples,” at which he, “by being sacrificed for his people, put an end to that, and all other ceremonial observances” (Gill). On the purifying, Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic rule — “every man is bound to purify himself for the feast” — with its varied measures of time according to the kind of defilement; the pilgrims went up early to cleanse themselves from ceremonial uncleanness (cf. Numbers 9:10) before keeping the Passover. Augustine, prefacing his homily, remarks that some passages of Scripture “are so clear as to require a hearer rather than an expounder,” passing lightly over the plain notice of the feast.

Henry, for the Puritans, draws the devotional lesson from the custom. “Before our gospel passover we must renew our repentance”; as these, “more devout than their neighbours, spent some time before the passover at Jerusalem” in purification, so “when we expect to meet God, we must solemnly prepare.” The witnesses thus find in this transitional verse both a note of time — the last Passover drawing near, toward which the whole Gospel has been moving — and a quiet contrast. While the multitudes scrupulously purify their bodies for the feast, the rulers plot the murder of the Lamb of God; the outward cleansing of many runs alongside the inward defilement of those who decree Christ’s death. The very Passover for which the people prepare will be the Passover at which the true Passover Lamb is slain.

The witnesses note the poignancy of the timing. The nation streams up to Jerusalem to purify itself for the Passover, scrupulous over every ceremonial defilement, while its leaders carry the unpurged guilt of a plotted murder. The shadow of the true Passover falls across the whole scene: the people prepare to eat the paschal lamb, not knowing that the Lamb of God is even now being marked for slaughter, and that this feast will be the last of the old order, fulfilled and ended in his death.
56:
“Then sought they for Jesus, and spake among themselves, as they stood in the temple, What think ye, that he will not come to the feast?” The witnesses describe the buzz of expectation among the festival crowds. The grammatical-historical commentators read the question as the curiosity of pilgrims who knew both of Christ’s fame and of the rulers’ hostility. They “sought for Jesus,” discussing among themselves whether, given the danger, he would dare appear; Ellicott and Robertson note the form of the question expects the answer that he will not come, the crowd doubting he would risk the feast after the decree (v.57). Gill pictures them standing in the temple courts, debating his likely course. The interest is general: the raising of Lazarus had made Jesus the talk of all Jerusalem, and his coming to the Passover, in the teeth of the Sanhedrin’s warrant, was the question on every tongue.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the scene the strange mingling of devotion and danger that marks the whole close of the chapter. The pilgrims who came to purify themselves now gather in knots to speculate about the man the rulers have condemned; the temple, made ready for the feast, hums with talk of the One whose death is already decreed. The witnesses thus read the verse as the hush before the storm: all Jerusalem is watching for Jesus, the authorities are armed with a commandment to seize him, and the city waits to see whether he will come. He will come — but in his own time and way, riding in as King a few days hence, to keep the Passover at which he himself will be the sacrifice. The expectation of the crowd is, unknown to them, the gathering of witnesses for the last great act.

The witnesses find in the crowd’s questioning a measure of how far the fame of Lazarus’ raising had spread. All Jerusalem is talking of Jesus; the only question is whether he will brave the rulers’ wrath and appear. The expectation is charged with both hope and dread — hope in those who longed to see him, dread of the warrant that awaited him. Into this watchful, divided city he will indeed come, but as the Lord of his own hour, to fulfil at the feast the purpose for which he had come into the world.
57:
“Now both the chief priests and the Pharisees had given a commandment, that, if any man knew where he were, he should shew it, that they might take him.” The witnesses close the chapter with the public warrant for Christ’s arrest. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the Sanhedrin, having decreed his death (v.53), now issued an open order that any who knew his whereabouts must inform, so that they might seize him (Gill, Clarke). This, Ellicott notes, accounts for the withdrawal to Ephraim (v.54): “He had heard of the decree… which had been publicly made known, and therefore avoided persons who would have carried it into effect.” Robertson observes that the commandment turns the whole nation into potential informers, and sets the stage for the betrayal — the means by which, through Judas, the rulers will at last lay hands on him.

The witnesses thus end the Lazarus narrative on a note of gathering menace. The chapter that opened with a sick friend and rose to the cry “Lazarus, come forth” closes with a hunted Saviour and a price upon his discovery. The same power that called the dead from the tomb is now, by the world’s reckoning, a fugitive, sought by the authorities of his own people. Yet the witnesses, with the Evangelist, read it all within the providence that governs the whole: the decree, the warrant, the watching crowds, the withdrawal to Ephraim — every thread is drawing toward the Passover at which Christ, of his own will and at the Father’s appointed hour, will give himself up. The hunters issue their commandment; but the One they seek will not be taken until he chooses to be found, that he may die the death by which he gathers the scattered children of God.

The witnesses note that the commandment makes the whole people complicit, turning neighbours into potential informers and preparing the way for the betrayer from within. Yet they read it, with John, as no triumph of the rulers but a stage in the divine plan. The warrant cannot hasten the arrest by an hour; the Saviour will keep his liberty until the Passover, and then surrender it freely. The chapter thus ends with the world arming itself against Christ, and Christ, unhurried and unafraid, awaiting the appointed time to lay down his life of himself.
